results.title: 20+ Hijacked Government Websites Became an Attack Channel
results.author: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)
results.content:
More than 20 Brazilian government websites were hijacked and turned into malware delivery channels in an active PhantomEnigma campaign uncovered by ANY.RUN, a leading provider of interactive malware analysis and threat intelligence solutions.
The investigation revealed previously undocumented backdoor behavior, hidden infrastructure relationships, and multiple attack arms behind a campaign putting banks and public agencies at risk.
By connecting hundreds of seemingly unrelated sandbox sessions, ANY.RUN researchers exposed the operation’s broader scope and showed how trusted .gov.br links and authenticated emails helped the activity remain hidden.
For the complete technical analysis, infrastructure details, indicators, and detection guidance, read the full PhantomEnigma investigation report
Trusted Government Infrastructure Became the Lure
The attack began with fake police-themed documents presented as official “Ofício Polícia Civil” or “Procuração Digital” notices. Some contained QR codes, while others directed recipients to links designed to look like legitimate government resources.
| Fake police-themed document analyzed inside ANY.RUN sandbox for full visibility into PhantomEnigma attack | 
In several cases, the emails were sent through compromised mailboxes and passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks. That gave the messages a stronger appearance of legitimacy than ordinary spoofed phishing emails.
Victims were then redirected through compromised .gov.br hosts or police-themed lookalike domains before reaching the malicious installer. The government systems were used as trusted delivery infrastructure, not necessarily as the final targets of the campaign.
Observed Government Hosts
Among the compromised systems observed during the investigation were timon.ma.gov[.]br, loginam.sesp.es.gov[.]br (state public security), aplicacao.cbm.mt.gov[.]br (fire department), prodoc.ap.gov[.]br, and others.
| TI Lookup query that involves compromised government hosts | 
These legitimate municipal, public-security, and judicial portals were used at different stages of the delivery chain. Several also appeared across more than one PhantomEnigma attack arm, helping researchers connect activity that initially looked unrelated.
PhantomEnigma’s Evolution: Two Paths to Harder Detection
| Timeline of PhantomEnigma’s malisious activity | 
The timeline shows one operation evolving along two main paths:
Delivery: PhantomEnigma moved from banking-focused activity in 2025 to abusing compromised .gov.br websites and email accounts in 2026. This gave the campaign a more trusted route to victims without confirming a new target group.
Arsenal: The malware evolved from a browser-extension banker into a modular Inno/Node.js backdoor capable of executing JavaScript and delivering additional payloads.
For security teams, this combination creates a serious visibility gap. Trusted infrastructure reduces suspicion, modular payloads can change after infection, and rotating C2 domains quickly make static blocklists outdated. Behavioral analysis and continuous threat hunting provide more reliable coverage as the campaign evolves.
From Trusted Email to Full Compromise: The PhantomEnigma Attack Chain
| The analysis process of PhantomEnigma inside interactive sandbox | 
Once a victim engaged with the lure, the campaign moved through a multi-stage infection chain:
- Phishing email: A fake police-themed or official-document lure reaches the victim.
- Trusted infrastructure: The link redirects through a compromised government host or police-themed lookalike domain.
- Malicious installer: An Inno Setup, MSI, or another installer starts the infection.
- Patched Electron application: Legitimate software loads a malicious index.js backdoor.
- Backdoor activation: The malware collects system data, establishes persistence, and connects to rotating C2 infrastructure.
- Second-stage delivery: The backdoor executes JavaScript or delivers stealers, loaders, RMM software, and other malware.
- Business impact: The infection can lead to credential compromise, unauthorized access, fraud, data exposure, and operational disruption.
What Researchers Found Inside PhantomEnigma’s Backdoor
The sandbox sessions exposed more than a simple downloader. Hidden inside a patched Boostnote and other applications was a modular index.js backdoor built to identify infected machines, maintain access, and deliver different payloads on demand.
Once activated, the backdoor could:
- Collect the victim’s computer name, username, and system details
- Create a persistent machine ID and read a campaign tag stored beside the installer
- Establish persistence through login settings
- Check for new commands every 180 seconds
- Execute JavaScript directly through eval()
- Download and launch executable payloads
- Communicate through multiple beacon formats across rotating infrastructure
This modular design allows the operator to change the final payload without rebuilding the entire infection chain. A system initially exposed to the same installer could later receive a stealer, loader, remote management tool, or another executable, making both detection and containment more difficult.
A Warning for Banks and Public Agencies
PhantomEnigma shows how attackers can turn trusted infrastructure into a detection advantage. A legitimate government domain, authenticated email, or clean file verdict may lower suspicion even when the infection chain is already active.
For banks and public-sector organizations, the risk extends beyond one compromised endpoint. Stolen credentials and persistent backdoor access can expose internal systems, sensitive data, and financial operations, while fragmented alerts delay containment.
Security teams should give employees a safe way to report suspicious official-looking messages and investigate them beyond the initial verdict. Catching the trusted lure early can prevent credential theft, additional payload delivery, and a wider operational incident.
Get PhantomEnigma IOCs, infrastructure findings, and detection guidance to strengthen threat hunting and response.
results.summary:
Researchers at ANY.RUN have uncovered the PhantomEnigma campaign, which hijacked over 20 Brazilian government websites to distribute modular backdoors and malware through highly convincing, authenticated phishing emails.

### Key Points
- Attackers compromised legitimate .gov.br domains, including police and fire department portals, to host malicious redirects and bypass security filters.
- Phishing lures utilized fake police-themed documents and official notices that successfully passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication checks.
- The malware evolved from a browser-based banking threat into a sophisticated, modular Inno/Node.js backdoor capable of executing remote JavaScript commands.
- Once installed, the backdoor maintains persistence, collects system data, and communicates with rotating command-and-control infrastructure every 180 seconds.
- The campaign’s modular design allows operators to deploy secondary payloads, such as credential stealers or remote management tools, after the initial infection.

### Why it Matters
This campaign demonstrates a significant shift in threat actor tactics by weaponizing trusted government infrastructure to evade traditional email and web security defenses. Organizations must move beyond static blocklists and implement behavioral analysis to detect these sophisticated, multi-stage attacks that exploit the perceived legitimacy of official communication channels.

results.title: Why Italy’s Genoa bridge collapse led to one of its biggest criminal trials
results.author: Urooba Jamal
results.content:
In the northwestern Italian port city of Genoa, one of the deadliest disasters in the country, in 2018, put the spotlight on Italy’s ageing infrastructure – and raised questions about whether the tragedy could have been prevented.
On Thursday, judges in Genoa are set to deliver a verdict in the first trial over the collapse of the Morandi road bridge, which killed 43 people when it gave way on August 14, 2018.
Dozens of defendants face numerous charges as families of the victims await a decision they hope will finally bring some measure of justice, nearly eight years after the disaster.
The 1,182-metre (1,293-yard) structure, which had been dubbed Italy’s “Brooklyn Bridge”, was designed by the architect Riccardo Morandi. Inaugurated in 1967, it went through major reinforcement work in the 1990s.
By the turn of the century, experts continued to warn that the structure was deteriorating, yet critical repairs were never carried out.
So what happened on that summer day in 2018, and why did the disaster lead to one of Italy’s largest criminal trials?
What happened to the bridge in 2018?
On August 14 of that year, at about 12pm local time (10:00 GMT), a large, central section of the Morandi bridge gave way to torrential rain, crumbling to pieces and falling some 45 metres (148ft) to the ground.
About 30 cars were on that section of the bridge, with the crossing a key highway between Italy and France and also crucial for nearby port terminals.
Local officials declared a 12-month state of emergency in Genoa and promised 5 million euros ($5.7m) to the rescue efforts.
What caused the bridge’s collapse?
Shortly after the tragedy, attention turned to two factors in the bridge’s disintegration: Its safety maintenance and its design.
Investigations later uncovered that the collapse was triggered by the rupture of the load-bearing cables inside the stay of the bridge’s ninth pillar, which were eaten away by a highly corrosive atmosphere during its 51-year lifespan.
Prosecutors have also alleged that the collapse was caused by years of missed, inadequate or falsified maintenance aimed at delaying necessary repairs for as long as possible while officials continued to pay dividends to shareholders.
According to Enrico Musso, professor of transport economics at the University of Genoa, the bridge was also bearing a volume of traffic that it was not designed to bear.
Some 50,000 to 60,000 vehicles were crossing the viaduct per day – a result of the city’s population rising in recent decades, Musso told Al Jazeera in 2018.
Defence lawyers, however, have argued that the disaster was a result of an original construction flaw at the top of stay cable number nine – the one that collapsed – which they said was impossible to detect and could not have been prevented by maintenance.
Who were the victims of the bridge collapse?
A total of 43 people died in the disaster. Most were Italian citizens, alongside some French, Albanian and Chilean nationals.
The victims included a family setting off on holiday, young French nationals who travelled to Italy for a techno music festival, and a couple returning from their California honeymoon with their children in tow.
That couple was Claudia Possetti, 47, and her new husband, Andrea, 48, who were passing through the bridge to return to their hometown of Pinerolo with their two children, aged 12 and 16.
Claudia’s sister, Egle Possetti, will be among the relatives present in the courtroom on Thursday to hear the verdict of the collapse.
Even eight years later, Egle remembers her sister as full of life – a woman who was a good person and loved her family deeply, she said. Her nephew was an avid mountain biker, and her niece a “beautiful” dancer.
Egle heads the Comitato Parenti Vittime Ponte Morandi, the committee representing victims’ families, which has been fighting on behalf of their loved ones. Thursday’s verdict has been long awaited, she said.
“We fight so much to the end of this trial to have justice for [Claudia], for her family, for all the [victims], and also for Italian citizens – because we need more justice for all – we need security,” she told Al Jazeera, stating that she hopes all groups responsible for the accident are finally held to account.
Who is on trial for the incident?
Fifty-nine people face a slew of charges, ranging from multiple manslaughter to undermining transport safety and making false statements. They have all denied any wrongdoing.
Italian prosecutors have been seeking an 18-and-a-half-year prison sentence for a key defendant – the former Atlantia CEO Giovanni Castellucci. The Morandi bridge was operated by Atlantia’s motorway unit, Autostrade per l’Italia.
Prosecutors have also been seeking prison terms ranging from two years and four months to 15 and a half years for the other defendants in the three-year trial, including employees and executives of the company that operated the bridge, the firm responsible for its maintenance, and the country’s Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport.
Castellucci is already in a Rome prison, serving a six-year sentence over another fatal incident in 2013 on a viaduct in southern Italy.
One of Castellucci’s lawyers has said the sentence sought by prosecutors was “unacceptable”.
“It is a frightening sentence, one you would expect to be sought in a murder trial, not in a case where only negligence is alleged,” Guido Alleva said last year.
Autostrade and its then-maintenance subsidiary, SPEA, had the case against them closed in 2022 after a judge approved their financial settlement.
The bridge’s collapse caused a dispute between Atlantia, controlled by the powerful Benetton family, and the government, which ended in 2021 with the sale of Atlantia’s controlling stake in Autostrade.
Prosecutors allege the defendants knew the bridge, which was built in the 1960s, was at risk of collapsing and that hasty decisions were made about maintenance in order to save money.
The bridge’s designer had recommended regular upkeep on the cement span to remove rust, especially due to the corrosive effect of moist, salty air from the nearby Ligurian Sea.
Infrastructure Minister Matteo Salvini told relatives of victims, residents and authorities at a 2023 commemoration of the incident that the people who plunged to their deaths were not victims of a flood or natural disaster.
Rather, he said, they were “victims of greed, of people who didn’t do their jobs”.
What were the early warning signs?
A 2011 report by Autostrade stated that the bridge was decaying due to high traffic, local media reported at the time of the incident.
In 2016, an Italian structural engineer had warned that the bridge’s design was problematic.
In April 2018, three months before the bridge collapsed, Autostrade had launched a 20,000-euro ($22,840) call for a “structural retrofitting” of the bridge, which would have included work on the collapsed section, according to Il Sole 24 Ore, Italy’s financial newspaper.
And even as early as 2006, newspaper reports cite the government and other local officials considering demolishing and rebuilding the structure – but the project was haughtily contested, mostly by residents of the area.
What happened to the collapsed bridge?
In August 2020, the city inaugurated a new gleaming structure, replacing the destroyed bridge with a more high-tech one, named the San Giorgio Bridge.
The curved underbelly of the new span, designed by famed Italian architect Renzo Piano, evokes the hull of a ship, in tribute to Genoa’s maritime history.
It also houses four maintenance robots running along its length to spot weathering or erosion, as well as a special dehumidification system to limit corrosion.
Families of the victims had refused to attend the bridge’s inauguration.
“We won’t be at the inauguration; we don’t want the tragedy to be transformed into a carnival,” Egle Possetti told news agencies at the time.
Have there been other bridge collapses in Italy?
The Morandi bridge’s collapse had raised concerns that there could be up to 10,000 bridges and tunnels across Italy at risk, according to Al Jazeera’s Natacha Butler, who had covered the 2018 event.
“In the ’60s and ’70s, there was a building boom – there was a lot of corruption. Substandard concrete was being used,” Butler said at the time, adding that it led to questions over the safety of infrastructure across the country.
Numerous bridge collapses have taken place in Italy before and after the incident; however, the Genoa bridge’s fall remains the deadliest by far.
results.summary:
Genoa judges are set to deliver a verdict in the criminal trial regarding the 2018 Morandi bridge collapse, which killed 43 people and exposed systemic failures in Italian infrastructure.

### Key Points
- The Morandi bridge collapsed on August 14, 2018, killing 43 people after a central section fell 45 meters during heavy rain.
- Prosecutors allege the disaster resulted from years of inadequate maintenance and falsified safety reports to prioritize shareholder dividends over repairs.
- Fifty-nine defendants, including former Atlantia CEO Giovanni Castellucci, face charges ranging from multiple manslaughter to undermining transport safety.
- Investigations identified the rupture of load-bearing cables, corroded by the coastal environment, as the primary technical cause of the structural failure.
- The bridge, which carried 50,000 to 60,000 vehicles daily, had been the subject of expert warnings regarding its deterioration for nearly two decades.

### Why it Matters
The verdict serves as a critical test for corporate accountability regarding public infrastructure safety and the oversight of privatized motorway operators. It highlights the broader national crisis of aging, poorly maintained transport networks and provides a long-awaited legal resolution for the victims' families.

results.title: Lest We Forget the Horrors: An Unending Catalog of Trump’s Cruelties, Collusions, Corruptions, and Crimes: June 2026: Atrocities 1014-1112
results.author: Emily Greenberg and Chase Bush-McLaughlin
results.content:
Early in President Trump’s first term, McSweeney’s editors began to catalog the head-spinning number of misdeeds coming from his administration. We called this list a collection of Trump’s cruelties, collusions, corruptions, and crimes, and it felt urgent to track them, to ensure these horrors—happening almost daily—would not be forgotten. Now that Trump has returned to office, amid civil rights, humanitarian, economic, and constitutional crises, we felt it critical to make an inventory of this new round of horrors. This list will be updated monthly between now and the end of Donald Trump’s second term.
These lists, along with everything McSweeney’s publishes on this site, are offered ad-free and at no charge to our readers. If you are moved to make a donation in any amount or subscribe to our website’s Patreon, please do. This will help support this project and our other work.
ATROCITY KEY
 – Authoritarianism  
 – Constitutional Illegalities, Collusion, and/or Obstruction of Justice
 – Environment
 – Harassment, Bullying, Retribution, and/or Sexual Misconduct
 – Lies and Misinformation
 – Musk Madness
 – Policy
 – Public Statements and Social Media Posts
 – Trump Family Business Dealings
 – Trump Staff and Administration
 – White Supremacy, Racism, Misogyny, Homophobia, Transphobia, and/or Xenophobia
June 2026
-   – June 1, 2026 – The Trump administration issued a rule narrowing the Medicaid work requirement’s exemption for people who are “medically frail,” making it available only to those whose conditions “significantly impair” their ability to work. The rule states that diagnoses including cancer, HIV/AIDS, and end-stage renal disease do not by themselves qualify for the exemption.
 
 
-   – June 1, 2026 – As Canada’s trade minister traveled to Washington for talks on the future of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement, Donald Trump reposted a Bloomberg article about Canada’s technical recession with the comment: “51st State!” It was his first public use of the phrase in months.
 
 
-    – June 3, 2026 – The New York Times reported that a previously undisclosed Department of Homeland Security inspector general report had found repeated mistreatment of detainees at Louisiana’s Winn Correctional Center, including an officer placing one detainee in a chokehold and another stabbing a detainee’s thumb with a pen. Investigators also said the facility later refused to provide complete video footage of some use-of-force incidents.
 
 
-    – June 3, 2026 – New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill said federal immigration officials again refused to allow her into the Delaney Hall detention center in Newark. The day before, New Jersey had sued the GEO Group, the private prison contractor operating the facility, after it refused access to state health inspectors, as protests outside continued in solidarity with a hunger and labor strike by detainees.
 
 
-   – June 3, 2026 – The Washington Post reported that the Trump administration had appointed pardoned January 6 rioter Elias Irizarry to the Defense Department’s Special Operations and Low Intensity Conflict office, which oversees sensitive special operations and counterterrorism matters. Irizarry had pleaded guilty to entering the Capitol through a broken window while carrying a metal pole.
 
 
-     – June 3, 2026 – Donald Trump signed an executive order stripping job protections from nearly eight thousand federal employees in policy-making roles, making it easier to fire career civil servants. A subsequent New York Times investigation found that the White House had privately pressured the Merit Systems Protection Board before a March ruling embracing the administration’s expansive view of presidential power over the civil service. As Trump signed the order, White House aide James Sherk explained that it would treat policymakers like private-sector employees: “If they’re messing up, then they can be removed quickly.”
 
 
-   – June 4, 2026 – Mamuka Artmeladze, a forty-three-year-old man from Georgia, died in ICE custody at Winn Correctional Center in Louisiana, becoming the fiftieth person to die in immigration detention during Donald Trump’s second administration. His death came days after ICE ended a policy requiring the agency to publicly report deaths occurring within thirty days of a detainee’s release.
 
 
-   – June 4, 2026 – Lawfare reported that at least ninety-seven January 6 defendants pardoned by Donald Trump had since been arrested for, charged with, or convicted of new crimes, more than twice the number previously documented. The study identified five cases in which Trump’s pardons allegedly enabled later crimes by releasing offenders who otherwise would likely have remained incarcerated, including one who was later sentenced to life in prison for child molestation and another who was convicted of grand larceny and burglary.
 
 
-   – June 4, 2026 – Donald Trump pardoned Stephen Buyer, a Republican who represented Indiana in the US House from 1993 to 2011. Buyer was convicted in 2023 for trading on confidential information about a T-Mobile/Sprint merger and Guidehouse’s acquisition of Navigant, including a tip from a T-Mobile executive during a golf outing, and was sentenced to twenty-two months in prison after the judge found that he had obstructed justice by giving false explanations for his trades.
 
 
-   – June 4, 2026 – After multiple musicians withdrew from National Mall concerts for America’s 250th anniversary, saying they had been misled about the event’s political ties, Donald Trump announced his own rally with Lee Greenwood and Christopher Macchio. Trump said the other artists had been told “to stay home,” promised a “Rally to end all Rallies,” and listed himself among the performers as “a fine and highly dignified gentleman known as, President DONALD J. TRUMP!”
 
 
-     – June 4, 2026 – Parents of transgender children treated at Mount Sinai Health System reported that the hospital had told them it would provide their children’s medical records to the Trump administration under a federal subpoena. The reports followed a similar demand made by the administration to NYU Langone for years of records on minors treated for gender dysphoria, as well as files on the clinicians who cared for them.
 
 
-   – June 5, 2026 – Speaking to farmers in Wisconsin as his trade and foreign policies drove up costs, Donald Trump told supporters, “I don’t need this. I got elected. What the hell do I have to be here for?” He added, “I could be home right now in the beautiful White House, enjoying watching somebody else on television talking.”
 
 
 Trump Addresses Wisconsin Farmers (WKOW)
 
 
-    – June 5, 2026 – A federal judge ordered the Trump administration to restart asylum and immigration processing after striking down policies that had frozen asylum grants and halted immigration benefits for people from thirty-nine countries, most of them in Africa and the Middle East. “The court is reminded” that immigrants are often told to “follow the law” and “do things the right way,” the judge wrote. “This case serves as a perfect example of immigrants doing just that.”
 
 
-   – June 5, 2026 – An auction for oil leases in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge ended with bids on only about 70,000 of the 689,000 acres offered, despite Donald Trump’s promises to extract “liquid gold” from a refuge he had called “the biggest find anywhere in the world, as big as Saudi Arabia.” No major international oil companies participated, and the sale raised just $3.7 million, nearly half of it from Alaska’s state-owned economic development corporation.
 
 
-   – June 5, 2026 – The New York Times reported that the Trump administration had drafted an order directing the US Forest Service to open millions of acres of national forest land to off-road vehicles, including about five million acres mostly in Idaho and Montana that the agency had recommended to Congress for wilderness protection.
 
 
-   – June 5, 2026 – A Washington judge dismissed the Kennedy Center’s lawsuit against jazz musician Chuck Redd, who canceled his annual Christmas Eve jazz concert after the Center’s Trump-appointed board added Donald Trump’s name to the building. Redd had hosted the concert since 2006. The court found that he had not signed the 2025 agreement the Center claimed he breached and noted that the free concert lost no ticket sales.
 
 
-   – June 5, 2026 – FBI Director Kash Patel fired five bureau employees connected to a 2023 internal memo about far-right extremists’ interest in “radical-traditionalist Catholic” ideology. An internal review had found that the memo violated professional standards but showed “no evidence of malicious intent.” After the firings, Patel posted, “This FBI will never infringe on religious freedom.”
 
 
-    – June 6, 2026 – The New York Times reported that the Trump administration began greatly expanding immigration court dockets to accelerate deportations, with some judges’ caseloads doubling or tripling. In one New Orleans courtroom, more than two hundred cases were scheduled over two days, and immigration lawyers said eighty-nine people were declared absent and therefore deportable. In Chicago, a judge addressed more than twenty immigrants at once and gave them twenty days to submit their cases in writing.
 
 
-    – June 7, 2026 – The New York Times reported that recent US intelligence reports expressed serious concerns that Israeli spy agencies had intensified efforts to eavesdrop on senior Trump administration officials involved in negotiations with Iran. The day before, NBC News had reported on the Pentagon’s decision to raise Israel’s counterintelligence threat level to its highest rating. Current and former US officials said senior Trump aides made themselves easier targets by conducting national security business on personal phones, flying on private aircraft, and declining support from US embassies abroad.
 
 
-   – June 7, 2026 – Trump stormed out of a Meet the Press interview with NBC’s Kristen Welker. Early in the interview, Trump became agitated when Welker questioned him about his fund for January 6 rioters. After Trump made incorrect statements about California’s gubernatorial election process, Welker asked him to supply evidence. “They’re crooked, just like you’re crooked, your press is crooked. And Meet the Press is crooked,” Trump responded. He later added, “You’re either crooked, or you’re stupid. You play right into their hands with this crap. You know that these elections are rigged. Your network knows that they’re rigged,” and walked out.
 
 
 Trump Argues with Kristen Welker on Meet the Press (WKYC)
 
 
-   – June 8, 2026 – Trump nominated Todd Blanche, his former personal defense lawyer, for attorney general. As deputy and then acting attorney general, Blanche fired numerous Justice Department employees, targeted Trump’s enemies with indictments, was involved in the mishandling of the Epstein files, and played a significant role in the administration’s controversial proposal to create a $1.8 billion fund for those claiming they were victims of government mistreatment, including Capitol attack rioters. “Todd Blanche has never stopped acting as Donald Trump’s personal lawyer,” said Stacey Young, founder of Justice Connection.
 
 
-  – June 8, 2026 – Knicks fans booed Trump during the NBA Finals at Madison Square Garden. After Trump announced he would attend the finals, becoming the first sitting US president to do so, intense security measures were put in place. Other ticket holders were forced to wait in line for hours and undergo airport-style security screening while the streets around Madison Square Garden were shut down. When Trump briefly appeared on screen during the national anthem, fans booed. Asked later about the boos, Trump said, “It was, I think, mostly cheers.” Trump was later filmed sleeping during the Knicks’ only loss in the series.
 
 
-   – June 8, 2026 – Somalian soccer referee Omar Artan was denied entrance to the US despite holding a diplomatic passport and visa. An anonymous Trump administration official alleged that Artan had associated with “suspected members of terror organizations,” an accusation that many Somalians felt was unfair. Last year, Trump issued a travel ban against Somalia and called its people “garbage.” Artan would have been Somalia’s first World Cup referee.
 
 
-    – June 8, 2026 – Activists began a fast and vigil in solidarity with detainees at two ICE facilities in Adelanto, California. The detainees at Adelanto ICE Processing Center and the Desert View Annex had been on a hunger and labor strike since mid-May over inadequate access to clean water, food, and medical care. Four detainees had already died at Adelanto between January 2025 and the beginning of the fast.
 
 
-   – June 9, 2026 – Social Security’s trustees said the program would reach a critical low by 2032, a quarter earlier than last year’s projection, due in part to Trump’s immigration and tax policies. This would mean the government would need to cut monthly benefits by 22 percent beginning in 2032. Over fifty-six million people rely on Social Security.
 
 
-   – June 9, 2026 – The Government Accountability Office found that Camp East Montana, an ICE tent encampment in Texas, endangered migrants and wasted millions. The GAO’s report cited loaded guns that went missing, people with HIV and diabetes left untreated, and cookies used in lieu of cleaning services. In the six months prior, three detainees died at Camp East Montana, including Geraldo Lunas Campos, whose death following a struggle with guards was ruled a homicide.
 
 
-   – June 9, 2026 – A ProPublica investigation revealed that the family of an Indian billionaire targeted by Trump secured major policy concessions from the Trump administration after investing in a company tied to Donald Trump Jr. The Trump administration had previously targeted the Ambani energy empire with tariffs. However, after Anant Ambani befriended Donald Trump Jr., his family made a nine-figure investment in America First Refining, a Texas startup in which Donald Trump, Jr. held partial equity. Around the time of the investment, the Ambani family secured major policy wins that benefited their company, Reliance Industries, including lower tariffs, a license to buy Venezuelan oil, and a sanctions waiver to buy Russian crude after the Iran war began.
 
 
-    – June 9, 2026 – Joint research by MS Now and the Marshall Project concluded that ICE had detained over five hundred babies and toddlers under Trump. After analyzing documents obtained by the Deportation Data Project, the journalists determined that ICE had twenty-five children aged three or younger in custody on an average day, a tenfold increase compared to when Biden was in office. Many children were held longer than the court-mandated twenty-day limit and went without proper nutrition, medical treatment, sleep environments, or toys. Pediatricians have said that detention at such a young age can have long-term consequences for a child’s health and development.
 
 
-  – June 10, 2026 – After US prices rose at their fastest rate in three years, Trump said, “I love it. The numbers were great. You know what I really love? I love the inflation.” According to Bureau of Labor Statistics numbers for May, prices rose by 4.2 percent in the past year, including a 0.4 percent spike from April to May due to the war in Iran. Trump later claimed his words were taken out of context.
 
 
 Trump on Latest Inflation Numbers: “I love it.” (KWTX)
 
 
-   – June 10, 2026 – Trump shared a clip from the television series The West Wing to justify new strikes against Iran. On Truth Social, Trump described the strikes as self-defense in response to Iranian aggression. He then posted a clip from The West Wing in which the fictional President Josiah Bartlet, played by Martin Sheen, argues that the US should hit its enemies with a “disproportional response,” leading to “total disaster.”
 
 
-    – June 10, 2026 – An immigration judge ordered the deportation of Mohsen Mahdawi, a Columbia University graduate student detained during the Trump administration’s crackdown on pro-Palestinian protesters. Mahdawi’s lawyers argued that their client could not be deported while his habeas corpus petition was still making its way through the courts.
 
 
-   – June 10, 2026 – Seeking to comply with Trump’s March 2026 executive order cracking down on mail-in voting, the Postal Service said it wouldn’t deliver mail ballots for states that refuse to hand over voter lists. Trump has repeatedly claimed without evidence that mail-in voting leads to voter fraud and sought federal control over elections, which is illegal. “This would deny eligible people the right to vote. Full stop. This is not in the president’s power. It’s absolutely clear in the Constitution—states run elections,” said Tobias Read, Oregon’s secretary of state.
 
 
-      – June 10, 2026 – Energy Secretary Chris Wright claimed that his department had not targeted Democratic states, despite DOE lawyers testifying to the contrary. “No decisions were made on politics,” Wright told a House committee. Meanwhile, his own lawyers had stated in filings related to the termination of eleven clean energy grants in states that Trump lost in 2024 that “a primary reason for the termination decisions at issue is because of location in blue states.” Two days after Wright spoke to the House committee, US District Judge Amit Mehta ruled that the grants must be restored.
 
 
 Chris Wright Spars with House Dems (The Hill)
 
 
-   – June 11, 2026 – A lawncare company run by Trump supporter James Hagedorn pledged to donate $1 million to repair any damage to the South Lawn caused by the UFC fight held on Trump’s birthday. Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) said the arrangement raised ethics questions. “Major corporations generally don’t do things out of the goodness of their heart. It’s generally they do things for the government because they want something from the government,” said Jordan Libowitz, CREW’s vice president.
 
 
-   – June 11, 2026 – Trump administration officials suggested a novel approach to paying medical bills: borrowing from health insurers. According to reporting by The New York Times, the unusual suggestion was included in a long, dense final rule issued in May on how the ACA will operate. Over a third of US households already have medical debt. “The last thing you want to do is to increase deductibles and load people up with more medical debt. It seems hugely out of touch with where people are,” said Neale Mahoney, an economist at Stanford University.
 
 
-   – June 11, 2026 – Seven states—Connecticut, Massachusetts, North Carolina, Oregon, Rhode Island, Vermont, and Washington—announced they would not participate in Trump’s “Great American State Fair.” While several states cited budget constraints and other resource constraints, others expressed concern over the event’s partisan nature.
 
 
-    – June 11, 2026 – Trump threatened to take over DC if Janeese Lewis George, a democratic socialist, won the Democratic mayoral primary. When asked about the campaign, Trump did not name George but appeared to reference her in his remarks: “I wouldn’t like it—and maybe we take back Washington, run it on the federal basis. We won’t put up with it. We’re not going to lose our businesses.” Trump does not have the legal authority to unilaterally revoke DC’s right to self-government.

With the new acquisition, Paramount will have 200 million subscribers. Paramount’s CEO, David Ellison, is the son of Larry Ellison, a longtime friend of Trump’s.
 
 
-   – June 12, 2026 – Federal agents searched the office of Ohio Organizing Collaborative, which supports expanded voting rights, criminal justice reform, and racial justice. As part of the operation, the organization’s staff were questioned, and documents and files were taken. Agents also visited the homes of staff members and questioned them about alleged voter fraud. “Any attempt to intimidate Ohio voters is wrong and will not work,” said Democratic Senate candidate Sherrod Brown.
 
 
-   – June 12, 2026 – Elon Musk became the world’s first trillionaire as his company SpaceX went public. An earlier Washington Post analysis found that Musk and his businesses had received more than $38 billion in government contracts, loans, subsidies, and tax credits at key moments. After donating $288 million to Trump’s campaign, Musk benefited from numerous Trump administration policies and was named head of DOGE, where he cut staff, budgets, and contracts at agencies investigating his companies. Oxfam estimated conservatively that more than 750,000 people had already died due to Musk’s cuts to USAID. The same day that Musk became a trillionaire, researchers claimed he had played an “instrumental” role in stoking xenophobic riots in Belfast earlier in the week.
 
 
-    – June 13, 2026 –  Less than a week after nominating Todd Blanche for attorney general, Trump nominated another of his personal lawyers, James McDonald, to serve as US Attorney for the Southern District of New York.
 
 
-   – June 13, 2026 – Data from sixty-two countries showed that global HIV prevention declined after cuts to USAID and changes to PEPFAR. Between 2024 and 2025, the number of people receiving PrEP dropped 38 percent, and condom funding dropped by more than 90 percent in some places. Approximately 1.2 million people contracted HIV, and around 570,000 people, including an estimated 94,000 children, died of AIDS or related illnesses in 2025.
 
 
-   – June 13, 2026 – Workers removed Trump’s name from the Kennedy Center. In late May, a judge ruled that the Center could not bear any name other than Kennedy’s without congressional approval.
 
 
 Workers Remove Trump’s Name from Washington’s Kennedy Center (Reuters)
 
 
-     – June 14, 2026 – President Trump celebrated his agreement with Iran, declaring on Truth Social, “Ships of the World, start your engines. Let the oil flow!” and claiming he had secured the “toll-free opening of the Strait of Hormuz.” The memorandum of understanding suspended tolls for only sixty days and left future charges to regional talks. Before the war, Iran had not charged tolls in the strait at all.
 
 
-   – June 14, 2026 – Donald Trump hosted the first professional sporting event at the White House, a UFC championship card held on the South Lawn that coincided with his eightieth birthday. The event streamed exclusively on Paramount+, whose parent company had received Justice Department approval days earlier for its $111 billion merger with Warner Bros. Discovery. Earlier that year, Trump had purchased between $15,000 and $50,000 in stock in TKO Group Holdings, UFC’s parent company. The event also featured World Liberty Financial, the Trump family’s cryptocurrency company, as an official UFC partner that awarded fighters bonuses in stablecoins.
 
 
 Watch the UFC Freedom 250 opening ceremony at the White House (Paramount+)
 
 
-     – June 15, 2026 – The New York Times reported, based on confidential White House memoranda, that the Trump administration had seriously considered suspending habeas corpus to accelerate the deportation of undocumented immigrants. The memos also showed internal alarm over Stephen Miller’s push to invoke the Insurrection Act against immigration protesters. White House staff secretary Will Scharf warned that both proposals would likely violate longstanding constitutional limits and provoke major legal battles.
 
 
-    – June 16, 2026 – Federal prosecutors charged fifteen people with conspiracy for allegedly coordinating resistance to Minnesota’s “Operation Metro Surge.” The indictment cited activities including organizing blockades, communicating over Signal, and what prosecutors described as “stalking” ICE agents by following them during enforcement operations. The charges came after prosecutors had already dropped eighteen of the previous thirty-six federal protest cases arising from the immigration crackdown, including one in which a judge described a charging affidavit as “false.” Meanwhile, the federal agents who killed Renee Good and Alex Pretti remained uncharged.
 
 
-     – June 16, 2026 – Secretary of State Marco Rubio invoked his foreign-policy authority against Colombian immigrant and progressive activist Beto Coral after Coral criticized Abelardo de la Espriella, a right-wing Colombian presidential candidate endorsed by Donald Trump. Rubio wrote that allowing Coral to remain in the United States would undermine “US foreign policy interests in Colombia’s democratic processes.” ICE arrested Coral the same day in Phoenix, as he returned home with his twelve-year-old son and their dog.
 
 
-   – June 16, 2026 – At the G7 Summit, Donald Trump said the United States had “nothing to do with” the war in Ukraine. “It has no impact on us, other than we sell weapons,” he said. “We’re thousands of miles away.” Trump had pledged during his campaign to end the war within twenty-four hours.
 
 
-    – June 16, 2026 – The Trump administration announced that it would transfer the Education Department’s special education programs to the Department of Health and Human Services and move enforcement of school civil rights laws to the Justice Department, further dismantling the agency without congressional approval. The change put special education under Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who said in 2025 that people with autism “will never pay taxes, they’ll never hold a job, they’ll never play baseball, they’ll never write a poem, they’ll never go on a date,” and later defended the transfer by arguing that special education programs “are health-related programs rather than particularly educated programs.”
 
 
-     – June 16, 2026 – The Justice Department joined a lawsuit against Evanston, Illinois, for its reparations program, arguing that housing grants for Black residents harmed by the city’s history of redlining and housing discrimination violated the Constitution’s Equal Protection Clause. Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon called the program “race discrimination, pure and simple.”
 
 
-   – June 17, 2026 – A ProPublica analysis found that nearly a year after Donald Trump signed his signature domestic policy bill into law, at least 776,000 children in twelve reporting states were no longer receiving SNAP benefits, despite Republican supporters’ insistence that its changes would not affect children or other vulnerable recipients. By February 2026, the US Department of Agriculture reported that 4.3 million fewer people were receiving SNAP nationwide than a year earlier.
 
 
-   – June 17, 2026 – A ProPublica investigation found that the Trump administration had conditioned billions of dollars in global health aid on African governments providing the United States access to national health data systems. Although the State Department declined to release the full set of agreements it had signed with more than thirty countries, ProPublica reviewed nine of the deals and found that one agreement gave the United States seven years of access to Uganda’s health databases. Privacy experts said the agreements did not clearly restrict who could use the data, how much could be collected, or whether private companies could profit from it.
 
 
-   – June 17, 2026 – At the G7 Summit, Donald Trump repeatedly compared his Iran agreement to the 2015 nuclear deal negotiated under Barack Obama, insisting his own agreement was superior. Trump claimed that Iranians had mocked Obama as “a stupid son of a bitch” and denied that the United States would contribute to a $300 billion reconstruction plan for Iran despite the agreement’s commitment to work with regional partners on such a plan. Of his own deal, he said, “Nobody knows what it is, but it’s very strong.”
 
 
 Trump Announces Signed US-Iran Peace Memo with Iranian Officials at G7 Summit with Macron (The Hill)
 
 
-   – June 18, 2026 – Human Rights Watch released a report concluding that the Trump administration’s “Operation Metro Surge” in Minnesota had resulted in unlawful killings, racial profiling, arbitrary arrests, excessive force, and other violations of protesters’ and detainees’ rights. HRW also found that nearly two-thirds of immigrants arrested had no prior criminal history in the United States; that during the operation, Minnesota’s National Alliance on Mental Illness logged more than double its usual volume of ICE-related suicide hotline calls; and that attendance in one of the state’s hardest-hit school districts fell by nearly a third.
 
 
-    – June 18, 2026 – Mother Jones reported that the Trump administration spent $527,226 to build a pedestal for a temporary statue of Caesar Rodney, a Founding Father who enslaved people, after adding the project to an existing federal contract without competitive bidding. The pedestal ultimately cost nearly twice as much as the government had estimated, with officials citing the administration’s July 4 deadline as the reason for moving ahead without seeking competing bids.
 
 
-   – June 18, 2026 – The New York Times reported that ICE planned to sell or transfer seven of the eleven warehouses it had purchased as part of the Trump administration’s mass deportation effort, abandoning more than $700 million in detention facilities roughly a year after buying them. The agency’s retreat followed environmental lawsuits, an inspector general investigation, and opposition from local communities, including Republican officials. ICE had spent nearly $1 billion acquiring the warehouses.
 
 
-     – June 18, 2026 – Trump claimed that the new White House ballroom was “on time, and under budget” and said it would include “ALL of the other many Military elements, which are all vital for National Security.” The same day, The Washington Post reported that the administration had redirected $352 million from the Secret Service to “White House Security Measures,” including security upgrades to the ballroom and the secure bunker beneath it. The funding came from money Congress had appropriated after two assassination attempts against Trump to strengthen the Secret Service, even as the White House insisted of the ballroom, “It’s all private donations.”
 
 
-   – June 18, 2026 – President Trump publicly mocked Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni, one of his closest political allies in Europe, claiming she had “begged” him for a photograph at the G7 Summit in France. “I wouldn’t have done it, but I felt sorry for her!” Trump said. The next day, Meloni responded that Trump’s account was “completely fabricated” and added, “I don’t know why the president of the United States behaves this way toward his own allies.”
 
 
-   – June 19, 2026 – Felix Alcorta-Rodriguez, a sixty-three-year-old Mexican man, died at the Webb County Detention Center in Laredo three days after ICE arrested him. ICE said that detention staff found Alcorta-Rodriguez unresponsive and that he was pronounced dead less than an hour later, after emergency responders took him to a hospital. His death was at least the fifth in a Texas immigration detention facility, and at least the twentieth in ICE custody nationwide, in 2026.
 
 
-    – June 19, 2026 – The Trump administration announced that it would end South Africa’s participation in PEPFAR, the US program that has supported HIV prevention and treatment across Africa for more than two decades. The administration said South Africa had failed to satisfy White House policy demands unrelated to public health, including repealing a land-expropriation law, exempting American companies from Black empowerment laws, and distancing itself from Iran. South Africa has the world’s largest HIV epidemic, with about eight million people living with the virus.
 
 
-   – June 19, 2026 – Trump presented the Boeing 747 donated by Qatar to the public for the first time, ahead of its service as the new Air Force One, despite concerns that accepting a roughly $400 million aircraft from a foreign government could violate the Constitution’s Emoluments Clause. Trump has said he intends for the plane to be transferred to his presidential library after he leaves office, potentially allowing him continued access to it after his presidency. Earlier proposals to donate the aircraft directly to Trump’s library were rejected because government lawyers concluded they would violate the Constitution’s Emoluments Clause. Trump called it “a flying White House at a level of luxury that nobody’s ever seen before.”
 
 
-   – June 19, 2026 – Hours after footage of former Olympian David Hearn being arrested at the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool circulated on X, Donald Trump blamed “Radical Left Lunatics” and “most likely Dumocats” for problems with the newly resurfaced pool. He claimed without evidence that someone had used chemicals “to try to destroy and demean our beautiful work” and accused ABC reporter Jonathan Karl of “trying to rip the rubber off of the surface.” The next day, Trump acknowledged the pool’s deterioration was so severe that it would likely have to be at least partly drained for “necessary repairs.”
 
 
-   – June 20, 2026 – Nolberto Armando Sanabria Meza, 46, died after being struck by a Park Police vehicle chasing a suspected stolen vehicle in DC. Under the Trump administration, the Park Police have relaxed their policies to allow for more chases.
 
 
-   – June 21, 2026 – Federal prosecutors dropped a criminal investigation into the commutation of a man convicted of fraud who allegedly lobbied and bribed individuals with ties to Trump. A private equity executive, David Gentile, was convicted in a $1.6 billion fraud scheme that resulted in the loss of retirement savings for thousands of retail investors; he was sentenced to seven years in prison, of which he served only two weeks, and ordered to pay $15.5 million. During their investigation into the commutation that wiped away Gentile’s charges and penalties, prosecutors found evidence that Gentile had discussed making millions in payments to secure his clemency and had been in communication with Reverend Frank Mann, a retired Catholic priest with ties to Trump. After The New York Times began inquiring about Gentile’s clemency, prosecutors were ordered to drop the case.
 
 
-  – June 21, 2026 – Amid speculation about Keir Starmer’s future, Trump announced on social media that the embattled British prime minister would resign—before Starmer himself had made any such public statement. Trump’s confident yet premature announcement stunned British political analysts. “There is literally no boundary this American president will not bulldoze through,” ITV’s Robert Peston posted on social media.
 
 
-    – June 21, 2026 – Abelardo de la Espriella, a right-wing candidate backed by Trump, narrowly won Colombia’s presidential election. After Trump endorsed de la Espriella, Congressional Republicans had encouraged Colombians living in the US to vote for him, and an activist critical of de la Espriella was detained by US immigration authorities. “The attempt to quash dissent in a coordinated manner with the Trump administration, extending from Colombia to US soil—I can’t think of anything in recent times, in the twenty-first century, that resembles that,” said Alexander Main, international policy director at the Center for Economic and Policy Research in Washington. On the night de la Espriella was declared victorious, some of his supporters were spotted wearing MAGA-style “Make Colombia Great Again!” hats.
 
 
-  – June 22, 2026 – Bill Pulte, the new acting director of national intelligence who has no background in national security, began firing staff. Democrats and former intelligence officers both expressed concern, particularly given that the department had already undergone significant downsizing in 2025. Days later, Pulte also named Christina Norton, a Republican political operative who oversaw a controversial poll-watching program and has no background in intelligence, as his chief of staff.
 
 
-   – June 22, 2026 – Cuba ended its school year two weeks early and canceled college entrance exams due to the fuel shortage caused by the US oil blockade. In February, the Cuban government was forced to cut the school day in half and begin remote learning for college students. “Education in Cuba is at risk due to the current energy crisis. It jeopardizes the future of an entire generation,” said Anne Lemaistre, the regional director of UNESCO.
 
 
-     – June 22, 2026 – Multiple federal judges ruled against the Trump administration. Judge Sparkle L. Sooknanan ruled that a recently upgraded federal tool for checking citizenship status could not be used, arguing that it would likely purge voters. Judge Amy Berman Jackson blocked state bans on using food stamps to buy soda and candy, a major setback for Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s “Make America Healthy Again” movement. And Patrick J. Schiltz dismissed Justice Department subpoenas issued to Governor Tim Walz and Mayor Jacob Frey over immigration enforcement in Minnesota. Later in the week, appeals courts rejected the DOJ’s attempts to gather state voting data and ruled that Biden-era air pollution limits should remain in place. Judge Indira Talwani also struck parts of Trump’s order limiting mail-in voting.
 
 
-   – June 22, 2026 – Trump baselessly blamed vandals after the Reflecting Pool’s lining separated from its bottom. “You know, we have 100… I think 290- to 300-foot slit right through it. Probably from a box cutter or a knife of some kind,” said Trump. However, National Park Service workers found cuts only on foam sections unrelated to the pool’s peeling blue coating. At the same event, Trump said the problems with the Reflecting Pool were not his fault, blaming vandals for the pool’s algae blooms and suggesting someone may have put fertilizer in it, though he did not provide evidence. “They did something to create the algae,” he said. A day earlier, three dead ducks were found floating in the Reflecting Pool. 
 
 
 Pool Owners React to Algae Bloom Turning Trump’s Reflecting Pool Green (AP)
 
 
-     – June 22, 2026 – Lawyers for the Southern Poverty Law Center argued that a letter from conservative groups to Stephen Miller may have prompted the Justice Department’s fraud investigation into the organization. The September letter, written by Moms for Liberty, Turning Point USA, and other right-wing groups criticized by the SPLC, used language nearly identical to an FBI document that established the investigation in October. SPLC had also published articles critical of Miller in 2019.
 
 
-    – June 23, 2026 – Seven anti-ICE demonstrators were sentenced to fifty to one hundred years in prison in connection with a protest last summer at the Prairieland Detention Center in Alvarado, Texas. During the protest, a dozen or so demonstrators had vandalized the property and set off fireworks. After a police officer pointed his gun at one of the protesters, another protester, Benjamin Song, then shot the officer, who was wounded but survived. Although the antifascist movement has no official structure or membership, the protestors were accused and eventually convicted of terrorism after Trump issued an executive order declaring antifa a “domestic terrorist organization,” though no such classification exists. The sentences were significantly harsher than those issued to individuals convicted in the Capitol riot attacks, which resulted in the death of US Capitol police officer Brian Sicknick. An eighth individual, who was not present during the Prairieland protest, was convicted of lesser crimes and sentenced to thirty years in prison, and another Prairieland protester was scheduled to be sentenced next month.
 
 
-    – June 23, 2026 – ICE agents tracked down a Syracuse poll worker, Paigelynne Gonyea, and ordered her to take down an Instagram post from January that they said threatened federal agents. The post included a photo of Jonathan Ross, the ICE agent who shot and killed Renee Good, with the following text: “BREAKING: The ICE agent who shot and killed Renee Good in broad daylight has been identified as Jonathan Ross by the Minnesota Star Tribune. I think today is a great day for Jonathan to be indicted!” Ross’s name and image were widely circulated in the press.
 
 
 Federal Agents Track Down Syracuse Woman, Demand She Remove Instagram Post About ICE (Syracuse.com)
 
 
-    – June 23, 2026 – The same ICE agents who approached Paigelynne Gonyea also visited the New York hotel of David Streever, who had sent an email criticizing how ICE had handled the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti to then-acting ICE Director Todd Lyons in January. In the email, Steever called Lyons a “monstrous human being” who would “never know peace” and predicted Trump would turn on Lyons but made no explicit threats. Steever, who lived in Rochester and had just returned from a trip to Finland, had not publicized his travel plans, suggesting that DHS may have tracked his credit card or other data. The agents had apparently visited his Rochester home days earlier.
 
 
-   – June 23, 2026 – Trump nominated tax attorney James R. Gadwood for chief counsel of the IRS. Gadwood worked for Miller & Chevalier, the firm that handled Trump’s taxes.
 
 
-    – June 23, 2026 – Trump admitted he’d asked a California US attorney to look into California’s gubernatorial election results and claimed, without evidence, that his efforts were instrumental in Republican Steve Hilton advancing to the runoff. “I called up the very powerful, very good U.S. attorney in California. I said, ‘Do me a favor. Take a look.’ … About an hour after the call, ladies and gentlemen, Mr. Hilton has won. Had I not made that call, Steve Hilton would be watching the election from home.”
 
 
-  – June 23, 2026 – The Washington Post revealed that the Justice Department had previously issued subpoenas to four national security reporters from the Post and The Wall Street Journal and then withdrawn them earlier in June after challenges from news organizations. The actions were part of the Trump administration’s ongoing crackdown on the Fourth Estate and First Amendment.
 
 
-   – June 24, 2026 – Trump’s Great American State Fair opened with power outages, low attendance, misspelled billboards, and a host of other issues. Numerous musical acts had already pulled out from the event, eleven states had declined to send official delegations, and North Carolina’s booth displayed images of the Confederate flag. Power outages caused the Ferris wheel to freeze, melting all the ice cream in the food hall. Trump also lied about the crowd size at his kickoff speech.
 
 
 The Great American State Fair Celebrates America, and Trump (New York Times)
 
 
-   – June 24, 2026 – Trump refused to sign a bipartisan affordable housing bill, the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, demanding that Congress first pass legislation requiring voter proof of citizenship. Later, Trump referred to the housing bill, which passed with overwhelming majorities in both chambers, as a “yawn.” “It’s so unimportant compared to the SAVE America Act,” said Trump. A May 2026 Gallup survey found that 15 percent of Americans consider high living costs the nation’s most important problem.
 
 
-  – June 24, 2026 – A federal judge ordered the government to stop seeking medical records of transgender youth who received care at New York City hospitals. “If not enjoined, the subpoena would allow the government to obtain and disclose the most intimate details of plaintiff’s medical histories and personal lives—in particular their transgender status,” said Judge Katherine Polk Failla, who argued that the government’s actions would cause “deep, emotional harm” and undermine trust in doctors and health institutions.
 
 
-  – June 25, 2026 – Gregg Phillips, the FEMA official who claimed he teleported to a Waffle House, was removed from his role leading the Office of Response and Recovery. Although DHS said Phillips was taking leave for personal reasons, sources told CNN he was pushed out. In March, CNN had reported on Phillips’s bizarre and inflammatory past statements and conspiracy theories.
 
 
-   – June 25, 2026 – JD Vance compared himself to Richard Nixon and downplayed the Watergate scandal, saying it would have been “like a twelve-hour news story” if it happened today. “The idea that it would have taken down a presidency is crazy,” said Vance. “Young senator, vice president, writes some best-selling books, is hated by the media. It kind of sounds like JD Vance. I’ve always liked Richard Nixon,” Vance added. Stoking conspiracy theories, Vance also blamed the “deep state” for Nixon’s downfall and said, “It’s not all that different from what the same groups of people, the same institutions, tried to do to Donald Trump in the first Trump administration.”
 
 
 VP Vance Says Watergate Would Now Be 12-Hour News Story and Crazy It Took Down Nixon’s Presidency (C-SPAN)
 
 
-     – June 25, 2026 – The Department of Interior confirmed that seven people were arrested, seven federal citations were issued, and eighteen police reports were filed in connection with alleged vandalism to the Reflecting Pool. One of the people arrested, former Olympic canoeist David Hearn, said he had only touched the pool’s detached liner. A Reuters photo also showed a police officer writing a citation for a teenager who had taken a piece of paint from the pool.
 
 
-    – June 25, 2026 – In a 6–3 ruling split along ideological lines, the Supreme Court said that the Trump administration could physically prevent asylum seekers at the southern border from crossing into the US, where federal law would entitle them to try to claim asylum and receive protections. In a lengthy and rare dissent from the bench, Justice Sonia Sotomayor said the court “blesses the executive branch’s decision to slam the door shut on all who are fleeing persecution, despite the detailed inspection and asylum system that Congress enacted and commands.” Immigrant advocates said the ruling violated international laws and agreements that the government inspect asylum seekers and ensure they are not sent back to dangerous conditions without an opportunity to appeal.
 
 
-    – June 25, 2026 – On the same day it ruled the Trump administration could block asylum seekers, the Supreme Court also ruled, in another 6–3 decision split along ideological lines, that the administration could end humanitarian protections for Haitian and Syrian refugees that have allowed them to live and work legally in the U.S. for over a decade. As a result of the ruling, approximately 350,000 Haitians and 6,100 Syrians were put at risk of deportation. Although the majority argued that the administration’s decision was not motivated by racial bias and did not violate Constitutional anti-discrimination measures, the liberal justices disagreed. In her dissent, Justice Elena Kagan quoted Trump’s repeated racist comments about Haitians, writing, “The statements fairly shout, in their racial undertones and overtones alike, that race entered the president’s resolve to remove Haitians from this country.”
 
 
-  – June 26, 2026 – John Bolton, who formerly served as Donald Trump’s national security advisor, pleaded guilty to mishandling classified security information. Bolton had retained the information to write his 2020 memoir, The Room Where It Happened, in an effort to profit off the dysfunction he witnessed in the White House.
 
 
-   – June 25, 2026 – Trump threatened 100 percent import tariffs on European countries that tax digital services from US companies. “Please let this statement serve to represent that any country that imposes such a tax will immediately be met with a 100 percent TARIFF on any and all Goods sent to the United States of America,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. The tariffs could trigger a major trade war between the US and the European Union.
 
 
-  – June 26, 2026 – After two devastating earthquakes hit Venezuela, eventually killing over 3,800 people, Trump claimed the country was doing well. “They had a tremendous earthquake, a lot of people killed… We’ve taken out millions of barrels of oil, and we’ve paid for the war many times over. But just as importantly… they’re making more money than they’ve ever done. Outside of what happened last night… it’s a happy country again, people are dancing in the streets,” said Trump.
 
 
 Trump: US Helping Venezuela After ‘Tremendous’ Earthquake, Calls It a ‘Happy Country’ Post US Action (WKYC)
 
 
-  – June 26, 2026 – The Religious Liberty Commission issued a draft report calling the separation of church and state a legal error and appealing to the Justice Department to promote “an originalist understanding” of how the Constitution views religion and government. The report also argued that faith-based groups working with the government shouldn’t have to accommodate civil rights laws; that public schools should allow religious displays, such as the Ten Commandments; that soldiers who refused vaccinations should be reinstated and financially compensated; and that non-profits should be allowed to make political endorsements.
 
 
-   – June 27, 2026 – Iran was eliminated from the World Cup after a tournament in which the United States limited the team to entering the country only on match days, forcing it to base itself in Tijuana and cross the border for games. Earlier in the tournament, Iranian officials said the United States had denied visas to fifteen staff members, including the head of Iran’s soccer federation and his deputy. After Iran was knocked out by another game’s last-second goal, its soccer federation called its treatment by the United States “unfair and unsportsmanlike.” Iran’s captain called the tournament “a disaster World Cup.”
 
 
 “It’s a Disaster World Cup,” Iran’s Football Captain Mehdi Taremi Says (Al Mayadeen)
 
 
-   – June 28, 2026 – A New York Times investigation found that while the Trump administration negotiated a deal giving an American company access to one of Kazakhstan’s largest untapped tungsten deposits, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump acquired financial interests in companies connected to the project. The Times also found that at least fourteen companies with ties to either the Trump family or Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s family had obtained or were seeking billions of dollars in federal support, including USA Rare Earth, which received. a non-binding commitment for $277 million in proposed federal funding and a proposed $1.3 billion federal loan.
 
 
-   – June 29, 2026 – A federal judge ruled that the Trump administration had violated federal law by freezing funding for the $16 billion Gateway rail tunnel project and barred it from attempting to suspend the grants again. Judge Jeannette A. Vargas pointed to Trump’s own explanation for the move: “We’re cutting a $20 billion project that Schumer fought for fifteen years to get, and I’m cutting the project.” The funding freeze had halted construction and briefly laid off about one thousand workers.
 
 
-   – June 29, 2026 – The Supreme Court declined to hear Donald Trump’s appeal of the $5 million civil judgment against him in E. Jean Carroll’s sexual abuse and defamation case. In 2023, a jury concluded that Trump sexually abused Carroll in a Bergdorf Goodman dressing room in the 1990s and defamed her by calling her accusations “a complete con job” and “a Hoax and a lie.” After the Supreme Court let the verdict stand, Trump again dismissed the lawsuit as “a Fake Case” and vowed to “continue the fight against this Weaponization and Lawfare Case against me, including the ridiculous claim of Defamation, with all of my power and strength.”
 
 
-     – June 29, 2026 – The Supreme Court ruled that Donald Trump could fire officials at independent regulatory agencies without cause, overturning a ninety-year-old precedent that had limited presidential control over agencies designed to operate at some remove from the White House. The Court made a narrow exception for the Federal Reserve, temporarily preventing Trump from removing Governor Lisa Cook over unproven mortgage fraud allegations because she had not been given an opportunity to respond. Trump hailed the broader ruling as a “Historic and Unprecedented” expansion of presidential authority and pledged to “take appropriate action immediately” against Cook.
 
 
-   – June 29, 2026 – The Trump administration agreed to pay Duke Energy $129 million to abandon plans for an offshore wind farm off the coast of North Carolina. It was the fourth time the administration had paid a company to surrender an offshore wind lease, bringing its total spending to more than $2.5 billion to stop offshore wind development.
 
 
-    – June 30, 2026 – The Supreme Court upheld laws in West Virginia and Idaho barring transgender girls from participating on girls’ sports teams, preserving similar restrictions in twenty-five other states. The Trump administration had backed the bans and previously directed federal agencies to withhold funding from schools that allowed transgender athletes to compete. Joshua Block, an ACLU lawyer representing the transgender students, said anti-trans groups had hoped to establish “a broad principle that transgender girls shouldn’t be treated as girls.” Trump celebrated the ruling on social media, writing, “The United States Supreme Court just RULED AGAINST MEN PLAYING IN WOMEN’S SPORTS. Wow! That takes that ridiculous situation off the table!!!”
 
 
-    – June 30, 2026 – The Supreme Court eliminated federal limits on how much political parties can spend in coordination with candidates, a ruling expected to benefit Republicans ahead of the midterm elections. The case was brought by Republican groups and by JD Vance when he was running for the Senate in 2022. After Trump returned to office, his administration reversed the federal government’s position and backed efforts to repeal the spending caps. Dissenting, Justice Elena Kagan warned that the ruling created “a legal regime increasingly unable to stop political corruption, and thus to preserve our institutions’ democratic legitimacy.”
 
 
-    – June 30, 2026 – The Supreme Court struck down Donald Trump’s executive order ending birthright citizenship for children born in the United States to undocumented immigrants and many temporary foreign residents, ruling that the Fourteenth Amendment guarantees citizenship to nearly everyone born on US soil. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote that the amendment promised “every free-born person in this land” the “right to have rights,” adding, “We keep that promise today.” Trump responded by falsely claiming Congress could “easily make it up… through Legislation,” even though five justices held that birthright citizenship was guaranteed by the Constitution.
 
 
 Trump Reacts to SCOTUS Birthright Citizenship Decision (CNN)

results.summary:
McSweeney’s has launched a monthly inventory tracking the policies, legal challenges, and controversies of Donald Trump’s second presidential term to ensure his administration's actions remain documented for public record.

### Key Points
- The Trump administration issued rules narrowing Medicaid work exemptions and stripped job protections from nearly 8,000 federal employees.
- Supreme Court rulings in June 2026 significantly impacted immigration, asylum processing, and the ability of the president to fire officials at independent agencies.
- Investigations revealed that ICE detained over 500 babies and toddlers, while reports surfaced regarding mistreatment at the Winn Correctional Center in Louisiana.
- Donald Trump pardoned several individuals, including former Representative Stephen Buyer, while facing ongoing scrutiny over family business dealings and potential conflicts of interest.
- The administration faced legal setbacks regarding the termination of clean energy grants and attempts to restrict mail-in voting procedures.

### Why it Matters
This ongoing documentation project provides a centralized, factual record of executive actions and their societal consequences during a period of significant political and constitutional tension. By cataloging these events, the project serves as a resource for researchers, journalists, and the public to monitor the long-term impact of the administration's policies on democratic institutions and civil rights.
